sourceTitle: Best practices for creating and optimizing a Custom Agent

questions.question: For which type of work should you use Notion Agent instead of a Custom Agent?

options.text: Recurring weekly reports
options.explanation: Notion Agent is included in your plan for one-time asks, while Custom Agents are designed for recurring work that runs on Notion credits.

options.text: One-time asks
options.explanation: Correct. Notion Agent is included in your plan for one-time asks, whereas Custom Agents are optimized for recurring work.

options.text: Tasks that run autonomously in the background
options.explanation: Custom Agents are specifically used for tasks that run autonomously in the background, regardless of whether you are online or not.

options.text: Tasks that require specific @mentions
options.explanation: While Custom Agents can be triggered by @mentions, Notion Agent is the correct tool for one-off requests.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to keep the context tight when setting up a Custom Agent?

options.text: To ensure the agent can access the entire internet
options.explanation: Keeping context tight restricts the agent to specific pages or databases to avoid reading unnecessary content, which saves credits and improves focus.

options.text: To reduce credit usage and keep each run focused
options.explanation: Correct. By pointing the agent to specific pages, it only reads what it needs to, reducing wasted runs and credit consumption.

options.text: To force the agent to use a specific AI model
options.explanation: Model selection is decided under the 'Reliability and Performance' settings, not by the scope of context.

options.text: To make the agent run faster
options.explanation: While running faster is a benefit of efficiency, the primary reason for tight context is to reduce unnecessary work and credit usage.

questions.question: What is the recommended model setting for most Custom Agents?

options.text: GPT-4
options.explanation: GPT-4 is a specific model. The article recommends the 'Auto' setting, which lets Notion intelligently pick the best model for the task.

options.text: Auto
options.explanation: Correct. The 'Auto' setting balances quality and credit usage by allowing Notion to select the optimal model for each specific task.

options.text: Enterprise
options.explanation: Enterprise is not a standard model option for Custom Agents; the 'Auto' setting is the standard recommendation.

options.text: Disabled
options.explanation: Agents must use an AI model to function; 'Disabled' would prevent the agent from running.

questions.question: When should you use a Schedule for a Custom Agent?

options.text: When a new email arrives from a specific sender
options.explanation: Triggering on specific emails is an example of a 'Trigger', not a Schedule. Schedules are for predictable work at a set cadence.

options.text: When the task should run at a set cadence regardless of activity
options.explanation: Correct. Schedules are ideal for recurring tasks like weekly reports or daily briefs that happen at a specific time.

options.text: When the agent needs to respond to a bug in Slack immediately
options.explanation: Immediate responses to Slack bugs are best handled by Triggers, as they run in response to an action.

options.text: When you want to read multiple databases at the same time
options.explanation: Reading multiple databases at once relates to 'parallel tool use' within a run, not the scheduling method.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an efficient instruction that uses parallel tool use?

options.text: Read the Projects database, then read the #support Slack channel.
options.explanation: This example is sequential (one by one), which is less efficient than reading independent sources simultaneously.

options.text: Read the Projects database and the #support Slack channel at the same time.
options.explanation: Correct. Since these sources are independent, reading them simultaneously reduces the number of steps and time required.

options.text: Create a summary page, then wait for a human to review it.
options.explanation: This is not an example of parallel tool use; it describes a sequential workflow involving a human in the loop.

options.text: Search the entire internet for information.
options.explanation: Searching broadly is discouraged; the article advises pointing the agent to specific databases or pages it needs.

questions.question: According to the troubleshooting section, what usually causes 'Failed runs'?

options.text: The agent is reading too much content
options.explanation: Reading too much content causes inefficiency, not necessarily a failure of the run itself.

options.text: A permissions gap or unclear instructions
options.explanation: Correct. 'Failed runs' typically point to a permissions gap (access issues) or unclear instructions.

options.text: The model is set to 'Auto' instead of a specific model
options.explanation: Using 'Auto' is recommended and generally does not cause failed runs; it helps balance quality and usage.

options.text: The agent is not batching tasks correctly
options.explanation: While batching helps efficiency, incorrect batching does not typically cause a run to fail entirely.

questions.question: How should you handle 'No action needed' runs when using high-signal triggers?

options.text: Delete the trigger immediately
options.explanation: You should not delete the trigger. These runs are normal and efficient, as the agent has correctly determined there is nothing to do.

options.text: Expect them as part of the process
options.explanation: Correct. 'No action needed' runs happen when the agent evaluates the trigger and instructions and finds no work to perform.

options.text: Turn off the AI model
options.explanation: Turning off the model would stop all runs, not just the 'no action' ones.

options.text: Report them as a bug
options.explanation: These are expected behaviors of a well-tuned trigger, not bugs.

questions.question: When troubleshooting 'Incorrect output', what is the first step to fix it?

options.text: Check the activity log for permission errors
options.explanation: Permission errors usually cause 'Failed runs', not 'Incorrect output'. 'Incorrect output' is specifically related to the instructions.

options.text: Add more pages to the agent's access
options.explanation: Adding pages helps with 'Missing data', not 'Incorrect output'. Incorrect output suggests the agent knows the data but processed it wrong.

options.text: Make the instructions more specific
options.explanation: Correct. 'Incorrect output' usually means the agent needs more specificity in its instructions to understand what is expected.

options.text: Change the trigger to run more frequently
options.explanation: Changing the trigger frequency addresses efficiency, not the accuracy of the output.

questions.question: Why is it better to start with a weekly schedule rather than a daily one?

options.text: To save money on credits
options.explanation: While saving credits is a benefit, the primary reason is to ensure the output is consistent and reliable before scaling up.

options.text: To reduce the number of 'No action needed' runs
options.explanation: Lower frequency actually increases the likelihood of 'No action needed' runs, but it helps validate the trigger logic first.

options.text: To confirm output is consistent and reliable before scaling up
options.explanation: Correct. Starting small allows you to verify the workflow works as intended before increasing the load or frequency.

options.text: To force the agent to use a specific AI model
options.explanation: The frequency of the schedule does not force model selection; that is set in the 'Reliability and Performance' section.

questions.question: What should you do if your agent frequently errors due to permissions (e.g., unable to respond in Slack)?

options.text: Change the model to 'Auto'
options.explanation: Changing the model does not fix permission gaps. The permission issue must be addressed directly.

options.text: Delete the instruction that asks it to respond in Slack
options.explanation: While you could remove the instruction, the article suggests adjusting the agent's access under 'Tools and access'.

options.text: Adjust the agent's access under Tools and access
options.explanation: Correct. If runs frequently error for permission reasons, you need to grant the agent the necessary permissions in its settings.

options.text: Expect this behavior and let it retry automatically
options.explanation: Frequent permission errors indicate a configuration error that needs to be fixed, not just tolerated.

questions.question: Which of the following is a best practice when writing instructions?

options.text: List every step the agent must take to get there
options.explanation: Listing every step is micromanagement. It is better to describe the outcome and let the agent determine the steps.

options.text: Start with the outcome you want
options.explanation: Correct. Focusing on what success looks like (the outcome) provides the agent with clear direction without dictating every step.

options.text: Use abstract descriptions instead of real examples
options.explanation: Concrete examples are preferred over abstract descriptions to help the agent understand the desired format and style.

options.text: Add complex boundaries to handle all possible scenarios
options.explanation: Instructions should be kept short and focused. Complex boundaries can confuse the agent; simple boundaries are more effective.
